text: Conduct a hazard analysis. Identify biological / chemical / physical hazards reasonably likely to occur in each step of the process. Determine which hazards are significant (require control).
Identify the critical control points (CCPs) — steps at which control can be applied AND is essential to prevent / eliminate / reduce the hazard to an acceptable level.
Establish critical limits for each CCP (e.g. internal temperature ≥ 165°F for poultry, pH ≤ 4.6 for canned goods).
Establish monitoring procedures: who / what / how / when. Continuous monitoring preferred (e.g. thermograph chart) over periodic sampling.
Establish corrective actions for deviations from critical limits: identify cause, bring process back into control, evaluate affected product, document everything.
Verify the HACCP system works: validation (initial proof CCPs control hazards), ongoing verification (calibration of monitoring equipment, end-product testing, internal audits).
Maintain records of: HACCP plan + amendments, CCP monitoring, corrective actions, verification activities, supporting documents (hazard analysis worksheets, validation studies).

Preventive Controls for Human Food. Requires a qualified individual (PCQI) to develop a food-safety plan including: hazard analysis, preventive controls (process / sanitation / supply-chain / food-allergen), monitoring, corrective actions, verification, supply-chain program, recall plan, recordkeeping.

Foreign Supplier Verification Program. US importers must verify that foreign suppliers produce food in compliance with US food-safety standards. Components: identification of FSVP importer, hazard analysis, evaluation of foreign supplier, approval, verification activities.

Produce Safety Rule. Standards for growing / harvesting / packing / holding of fresh produce: ag water quality, biological soil amendments, sprouts, domesticated and wild animals, worker training and health/hygiene, equipment / tools / buildings.

Sanitary Transportation of Human and Animal Food. Requirements for shippers, loaders, carriers, receivers using motor or rail vehicles to transport food. Vehicles + equipment must be designed + maintained to prevent contamination.

Mitigation Strategies to Protect Food Against Intentional Adulteration. Required for large food facilities. Components: vulnerability assessment, mitigation strategies, monitoring, corrective actions, verification, recordkeeping, training.

FSMA Section 204 — Food Traceability Final Rule. Requires additional traceability records for foods on the Food Traceability List (FTL). Critical Tracking Events (CTEs): harvesting, cooling, initial packing, shipping, receiving, transformation. Key Data Elements (KDEs) must accompany each event. Compliance date Jan 2026.

Class I: reasonable probability of serious adverse health consequences or death (e.g. undeclared allergen, pathogen, foreign object). Class II: may cause temporary or medically-reversible adverse consequences (e.g. quality defect with possible illness). Class III: unlikely to cause adverse health consequences (e.g. labeling violation, minor undeclared minor-ingredient).

SQF Food Safety Code Edition 9. GFSI-recognized scheme. Modules: System Elements (management commitment, document control, internal audit), Good Manufacturing Practices (GMPs), Food Safety Plan (HACCP), Food Defense, Food Fraud.

BRCGS Global Standard for Food Safety Issue 9. Sections: senior-mgmt commitment, food safety plan + HACCP, food safety + quality management system, site standards, product control, process control, personnel, high-risk / high-care zones, traded product.
